title: 08 · Vision-language models
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 08 · Vision-language models
text:
The task
Every earlier module solved one capability with a purpose-built model. A vision-language model (VLM) — a model that takes an image (and text) and produces text: captions, answers, reasoning — collapses many of them into a single network you steer with a prompt (the instruction or question you give the model in plain language). Ask it to caption, answer a question, count, read text, or reason about what happens next — no task-specific head, no retraining. This is the frontier of “general” computer vision and the closest thing here to understanding a scene rather than just labelling it.

title: 08 · Vision-language models
text:
How it works
A VLM bolts a vision encoder (the part that turns the image into tokens the language model can read) onto a large language model (LLM) — a model that generates text, like ChatGPT; a VLM simply attaches a vision encoder to one. Image patches are projected into the LLM’s token space — a token being a small chunk of text (or image) the model processes one at a time — so the model “sees” the picture as a sequence of tokens and then generates text conditioned on both the image and your question (generative means it produces free-form output, here text, rather than picking from a fixed list of classes). Because the output is language, the same model can do tasks that would each need a separate architecture in the classic stack — the prompt selects the behaviour. For how VLMs relate to the other model families in this curriculum, see Model families.






TipA note on the model choice



The natural pick here was Microsoft’s Florence-2, a tiny unified vision-language model that you steer with explicit task tokens (<CAPTION>, <OD>, <OCR>). Its custom modelling code doesn’t load under transformers v5 (a config incompatibility), so we use Qwen2.5-VL 3B — a small, transformers-native generative VLM (the “3B” means 3 billion parameters, the model’s adjustable internal numbers) — instead. The lesson (one model, many tasks via prompts) is the same; only the interface differs (free-form chat vs task tokens).

One model answers caption/reason/count/OCR purely from the prompt — no task-specific heads. Latency is per short answer on NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti | 24.0 GB | sm_86 | torch 2.11.0+cu128 / CUDA 12.8 and scales with the number of generated tokens. See the transcript for real outputs.






NoteWhat to notice




One model, four capabilities. Captioning, open-ended reasoning, counting, and OCR all came from a single network — selected entirely by the prompt. That generality is the whole point of a VLM.
Grounded and mostly correct. Counts (0 / 4 / 5 people) match the scenes; OCR (reading text that appears in an image) correctly reports “none” when there’s no text; captions are accurate. It reasons about what might happen next — something a classifier or detector simply cannot do.
Generality has a price: latency. Latency is the time to produce an answer — here measured in seconds, far slower than the other modules. At ~3.7 s per short answer, this 3B VLM is ~100× slower than the YOLO detector. VLMs are the heavyweight of the curriculum — you reach for them when you need language-level flexibility, not real-time throughput.
Confident but fallible. Free-form generation can hallucinate (confidently state details not actually in the image); outputs should be treated as strong suggestions, not ground truth.

title: 08 · Vision-language models
text:
Where VLMs fail

Hallucination — fluent text can assert objects/attributes that aren’t there.
Precise spatial / counting tasks — better than older models, still unreliable in clutter.
Fine OCR & small text — general VLMs trail dedicated OCR systems on dense documents.
Latency & cost — orders of magnitude heavier than the specialist models in modules 01–07.

title: 07 · Embeddings & retrieval
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 07 · Embeddings & retrieval
text:
The task
Most of this curriculum predicts labels. This module predicts a vector: an embedding that places similar images near each other in a high-dimensional space. (An embedding or vector is just a list of numbers summarising an image so that similar images get similar lists — and retrieval means finding the most similar images to a query by comparing those embeddings.) That single idea underpins reverse image search, deduplication, clustering, recommendation, anomaly detection, and few-shot recognition — none of which need a classifier head. To “classify” you just look at your neighbours.
The workflow (the gallery is the searchable set of images; a query is the image you search with):

Embed every gallery image to an L2-normalised vector (L2-normalised = scaled so each vector has length 1, which makes the similarity comparison clean).
Embed a query the same way.
Rank gallery vectors by cosine similarity (a dot product — both are simple ways to measure how alike two vectors/embeddings are) and return the nearest.

title: 07 · Embeddings & retrieval
text:
Two kinds of embedding
We compare two backbones — the network body that turns an image into its embedding. DINOv2 (built on the earlier DINO) is self-supervised: trained without any human labels, e.g. by making different views of the same image agree. SigLIP 2 is image-text (contrastive): trained on image–caption pairs so an image’s vector lands near its caption’s. For more on where these models come from, see Model families.








Model
Trained on
Signal it captures




DINOv2
Images only, self-supervised (no labels)
Visual structure, parts, texture, shape — emerges purely from the data


SigLIP 2
Image–text pairs, contrastive
Semantics aligned to language — “what you’d call it”



DINOv2 learns by making different augmented views of the same image agree; it never sees a label, yet produces features so strong that a linear probe rivals supervised training. SigLIP learns from captions, so its space is organised the way language groups things. Both give excellent retrieval — the right choice depends on whether you want visual or semantic similarity.

title: 07 · Embeddings & retrieval
text:
Results
A real metric, not just pictures: we embed a balanced Imagenette gallery and measure how often the nearest neighbours share the query’s class — using no labels at search time.

Latency on NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti | 24.0 GB | sm_86 | torch 2.11.0+cu128 / CUDA 12.8. Figure uses DINOv2 Base.






Figure 1: Each row: a query (black border) and its 5 nearest neighbours. Green = same class, red = different. The model was never trained to classify Imagenette — this is pure embedding similarity.









NoteWhat to notice




Search beats training, for free. Both embedders retrieve the correct class >99% of the time (Recall@1 — did the single nearest image share the query’s class; a related measure, Precision@5, asks what fraction of the 5 nearest do) with no classifier and no labels at query time — add a new class by just dropping its images into the gallery.
Self-supervised is competitive with image-text. DINOv2 (never saw a single label or caption) trails SigLIP 2 by only a few tenths of a point here — a striking result for a model trained purely on images.
This is how you’d handle an open, growing catalogue — products, faces, defects — where retraining a classifier per new item is impractical.

title: 07 · Embeddings & retrieval
text:
Where retrieval fails

Fine-grained distinctions — near-duplicate categories (specific dog breeds, product variants) can blur in a general embedding; specialised/fine-tuned embeddings help.
Gallery quality & scale — retrieval is only as good as what’s indexed; at millions of items you need an ANN index (FAISS) — fast approximate nearest-neighbour search for large galleries — and recall/speed trade-offs appear.
Domain gap — embeddings trained on web images underperform on medical, satellite, or industrial imagery without adaptation.

title: 06 · Multi-object tracking
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 06 · Multi-object tracking
text:
The task
A detector (the model that finds objects and draws a bounding box — the rectangle around each one) answers “what’s here right now” — independently, every frame, with no memory. Tracking (also called multi-object tracking, or MOT — following the same objects across video frames so each keeps a stable id) adds the missing dimension: identity over time, so the same person keeps the same id (the persistent number identifying one tracked object across frames — also called a tracker id) as they move. That id is what lets you count, measure dwell time, analyse motion, or have a robot follow a specific person.
The dominant recipe is tracking-by-detection (run a detector each frame, then link this frame’s boxes to the tracks you already have):

Run a detector on each frame (here: YOLO26 — a fast, widely used object-detection model; see Model families — person class only).
Associate this frame’s boxes with the existing tracks — i.e. decide which new box belongs to which existing track — matching by predicted position (a Kalman / motion model: a simple predictor of where a tracked object will move next) and box overlap.
Births/deaths: unmatched detections start new tracks; tracks unseen for a while are retired.

ByteTrack is the associator. (It is an algorithm — a fixed set of matching rules — not a neural network that learns; see Model families.) Its trick is to use low-confidence detections during association (not just high-confidence ones), which recovers objects through brief occlusion (when an object is temporarily hidden behind something) and reduces dropped tracks.

title: 06 · Multi-object tracking
text:
Result






Figure 1: Detection + ByteTrack with motion traces. Each box carries a persistent id (#n); the tails show recent trajectory.

Multi-object tracking







Metric

YOLO26 (person class) + ByteTrack association, with motion traces. Qualitative + throughput; MOT metrics (HOTA/MOTA/IDF1 via TrackEval) on a labelled benchmark are a deeper follow-up.






Figure 2: Three frames across the clip — note how ids persist as people move.









NoteWhat to notice




Identity is the product, not boxes. The same code as module 01 produces the boxes; the value here is that box #7 in frame 0 is the same person as box #7 in frame 100.
More ids than people = fragmentation. This 200-frame clip yielded ~60 ids for far fewer actual pedestrians. When someone is occluded and reappears, the tracker often issues a new id — the classic ID-switch / fragmentation failure (a tracked object wrongly getting a new id, e.g. after being hidden) that HOTA and IDF1 are designed to penalise. (HOTA, MOTA and IDF1 are standard tracking scores for how well ids stay correct over time.)
Tracking is detector-bound. Every missed detection is a gap the associator must bridge; a better detector usually means a better tracker, for free.

title: 06 · Multi-object tracking
text:
Where tracking fails

Long / full occlusion — a person hidden for many frames usually returns with a new id.
Crowds & similar appearance — ByteTrack uses motion + overlap, not appearance, so it can swap ids between people who cross paths (appearance-based trackers like Deep OC-SORT help).
Fast motion / low frame-rate — the Kalman prediction drifts and association breaks.
Detector misses — propagate directly into track gaps.

title: 06 · Multi-object tracking
text:
Reproduce
Here supervision is a Python helper library that bundles ready-made tracking and annotation tools (including a ByteTrack implementation); see Model families.
uv sync --group detection      # ultralytics + supervision (ByteTrack)
uv run python modules/06-tracking/run.py
(The sample clip is downloaded by supervision’s asset helper into data/.)

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
The task
Pose estimation — locating a person’s body joints and connecting them into a skeleton — locates a set of body keypoints per person. A keypoint is a single labelled point such as “left elbow.” For the COCO format, there are 17 of them (eyes, ears, shoulders, elbows, wrists, hips, knees, ankles), connected into a skeleton. It powers motion capture, sports analytics, gesture interfaces, and human-aware robotics (a robot that must not bump the person reaching past it).

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
Two paradigms
There are two broad strategies. In bottom-up estimation, one network finds all the people and all their joints in a single pass; top-down instead first detects each person, then runs a separate pose network on each one. A single-stage model does the whole job in one network pass. The models named below — YOLO-pose (a fast, single-stage pose model from the YOLO family of real-time detectors), plus the top-down ViTPose and RTMPose — are described in more detail under Model families.








Paradigm
How
Trade-off




Bottom-up / single-stage (YOLO-pose here)
One network predicts people and their keypoints in a single pass
Constant cost regardless of crowd size; fast


Top-down (ViTPose, RTMPose)
Detect each person, then run a dedicated pose net on each crop
Usually more accurate, but cost grows with the number of people



This module benchmarks two YOLO-pose generations (single-stage): YOLO11-pose (the prior generation) and YOLO26-pose (the newer one). The top-down family is the accuracy-first alternative when you have few people and need precise joints.

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
How it works
YOLO-pose extends the detector: alongside each person box it regresses the 17 keypoint coordinates (and a per-keypoint visibility — a confidence that the joint is actually visible rather than hidden). Because keypoints are predicted jointly with detection in one forward pass, throughput stays high even in crowds — you can see this in the figures, where several skeletons are recovered at once.

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
Results
The table below reports speed two ways: latency, the time to process one image in milliseconds, and FPS (frames per second), how many images per second the model handles — around 30 FPS or higher counts as real-time. The footnote mentions OKS-AP: OKS (object keypoint similarity) is the pose world’s answer to IoU — a score for how closely predicted joints match the true ones — and is used to compute pose accuracy (AP).

NoteWhat to notice




Crowd-constant speed. Both models stay at ~50 fps whether the image has one person or several — the single-stage win. A top-down model would slow down as people are added.
A newer generation recovers more people. YOLO26-pose found more people per image than YOLO11-pose here (better recall on partially-occluded figures — i.e. people partly hidden behind something), at a slightly lower frame-rate.
Keypoints carry visibility. Occluded joints are predicted with low confidence; downstream code should use that, not assume every joint is reliable.

title: 05 · Human pose estimation
text:
Where pose estimation fails

Occlusion & truncation — joints hidden behind objects or cropped out of frame.
Crowding & overlap — skeletons can swap limbs between nearby people.
Unusual poses — gymnastics, lying down, or atypical viewpoints under-represented in training.
Small / distant people — too few pixels to localise joints precisely.

text:
Reproduce
uv sync --group detection
uv run python modules/05-pose/run.py --images 4

title: 04 · Monocular depth
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 04 · Monocular depth
text:
The task
Monocular depth estimation predicts a distance for every pixel from one ordinary RGB image — no stereo pair, no LiDAR. (Monocular depth just means estimating how far away each pixel is from a single image — no two-camera stereo pair and no laser/LiDAR sensor.) It’s the perception capability most directly relevant to robotics: turning a flat photo into 3D structure for navigation, grasping, and obstacle avoidance.
A key distinction (relative vs metric depth: relative orders pixels near-to-far without real units, while metric gives actual metres):

Relative depth — orders pixels near-to-far without real-world units. Enough for “what’s in front of what”. Depth Anything V2 predicts this.
Metric depth — actual metres. Needed for measurement/control; harder, and usually fine-tuned per camera (e.g. Depth Anything’s metric heads, Apple Depth Pro). Depth Anything V2 is a popular open model for predicting depth from a single image; Depth Pro (from Apple) is a model aimed at metric depth. (See Model families for how these fit among other vision models.)

title: 04 · Monocular depth
text:
How it works
Depth Anything V2 is a DINOv2 vision-transformer encoder plus a lightweight depth head, trained on a huge mix of labelled + pseudo-labelled images. (A transformer is a network architecture that relates all parts of an image to each other through “attention”; a Vision Transformer (ViT) applies this by splitting the image into small patches. The encoder here acts as the backbone — the main body of the network that turns an image into useful features.) The transformer’s global receptive field lets it use scene-wide cues — perspective, relative size, texture gradients, occlusion — exactly the monocular cues (depth hints in a single image, like perspective, relative size, and occlusion) humans use to perceive depth with one eye closed. It outputs inverse depth — a representation where larger values mean nearer.

title: 04 · Monocular depth
text:
Results
We run two sizes on the same images. Larger backbones generally produce sharper boundaries and better thin-structure handling, at a latency cost. (Latency is the time to process one image, usually in milliseconds; FPS — frames per second — is how many images it handles per second, where roughly 30 FPS counts as real-time.)

Figure 1: RGB input vs predicted depth (brighter = nearer). Note how both models recover ground-plane gradient, object separation, and rough scene layout from a single photo.









NoteWhat to notice




Real 3D structure from one image. With no stereo or sensor, the model separates foreground from background, recovers the ground plane, and orders objects in depth — the cue set a robot can turn into a rough 3D map.
Size buys sharpness, not speed. Depth Anything V2 Base (~13 fps) gives crisper edges than Small (~26 fps); the right pick depends on whether you need real-time or best quality.
Relative, not metric. These maps tell you nearer/farther, not how many metres — for control you’d use a metric variant calibrated to your camera.

title: 04 · Monocular depth
text:
Where monocular depth fails

Absolute scale ambiguity — a doll-house and a real house look identical to a single camera; relative depth can’t resolve true size.
Reflective / transparent surfaces (mirrors, glass, water) — violate the visual cues the model relies on.
Untextured walls & sky — few gradients to reason from.
Out-of-distribution cameras — fisheye, aerial, or endoscopic views differ from the training mix and degrade.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
The task
Segmentation assigns labels at the pixel level rather than the box level. In plain terms, segmentation means labelling every pixel — saying which object each dot of the image belongs to — instead of just drawing a bounding box (a rectangle around an object). The output is a mask: a per-pixel yes/no marking of exactly which pixels belong to a given object. Two flavours matter here:

Instance segmentation — a separate mask per object (two people → two masks), each with a class. This is detection + a mask head. (Instance segmentation separates individual objects; semantic segmentation, by contrast, labels pixels by category only — all people become one “person” blob — without telling them apart.)
Promptable segmentation — “segment whatever I point at / box / name”, with no fixed class set. Promptable means you steer the model with a prompt — a click, a box, or text — rather than picking from a fixed class list. This is the foundation-model approach (a large model pretrained broadly and reused for many tasks, often just by prompting), embodied by Segment Anything. For a map of the model families mentioned below, see Model families.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
Two paradigms
Two very different model families show up below. YOLO-seg (the segmentation variant of the fast, popular YOLO object detectors — here the YOLO26-seg and YOLO11-seg versions) is quick and always tells you what each mask is. SAM 2.1 (Segment Anything Model 2.1, a promptable segmentation foundation model from Meta) is slower but will outline almost anything you point at. SAM is class-agnostic: it produces masks without saying what the object is.









Model
Approach
Label set
Trait




YOLO26-seg / YOLO11-seg
Closed-set instance segmentation
80 COCO classes
Fast; every mask comes with a class label


SAM 2.1
Promptable segmentation
none — class-agnostic
Segments anything from a point/box/mask prompt; no labels



The natural way they combine is the detect-then-segment pipeline: a detector proposes boxes, and SAM turns each box into a high-quality mask. We use exactly that here — SAM 2.1 is prompted with the YOLO26-seg boxes.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
How each works

YOLO-seg adds a mask-prototype head to the detector: it predicts a handful of prototype masks per image plus per-object coefficients that linearly combine them — cheap, hence the high frame-rate.
SAM 2.1 is a promptable transformer with a heavy image encoder and a light mask decoder. Encode the image once, then any prompt (point, box) decodes to a mask in milliseconds. It knows nothing about classes — it only knows “object-ness”, which is why it generalises to things no COCO model has names for.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
How we measured
This module is qualitative + latency (warmed-up single-image forwards on the local RTX 3090 Ti); full mask-mAP on COCO is a deeper follow-up. Latency is the time to process one image (in milliseconds), often reported as FPS (frames/images per second — roughly 30 means real-time). mask mAP is the usual detection score (mAP) computed using mask overlap instead of box overlap; the standard pixel-level segmentation score you may also meet elsewhere is mIoU (mean Intersection-over-Union — the average pixel overlap between prediction and truth, across classes). The point here is to see the difference between a fast labelled instance segmenter and a slower, class-agnostic, promptable one.

Labels vs flexibility. YOLO-seg runs at ~43 fps and hands you a class with every mask — but only for its 80 COCO categories. SAM 2.1 is ~4× slower (~11 fps) and returns no label, yet will segment anything you can point at, including objects no detector was trained on.
They compose. The figures show the standard pattern: the detector finds and names objects; SAM refines each box into a crisp mask. Open-vocab detection (module 01) + SAM gives you “segment anything you can describe in words”.
Mask quality differs from box quality. A correct box with a sloppy mask is a common failure; SAM’s masks are typically tighter around object boundaries than a fast instance head’s.

title: 03 · Segmentation
text:
Where segmentation fails

Thin / wispy structures (hair, wires, chain-link) — hard for any mask head.
Overlapping instances — instance segmenters merge or split touching objects.
Ambiguous prompts — a single SAM point can mean “the shirt” or “the whole person”; SAM resolves ambiguity by returning multiple candidate masks.
Class limits (instance) — YOLO-seg simply can’t mask a class it never trained on.

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
The task
Image classification assigns a single label to a whole image — the oldest and most foundational CV task, and the one the deep-learning era was launched on (AlexNet on ImageNet, 2012). Here it’s also the cleanest place to see the field’s biggest recent shift, because the same benchmark can be attacked two completely different ways.
We evaluate on Imagenette — a 10-class, Apache-licensed subset of ImageNet (tench, English springer, cassette player, chainsaw, church, French horn, garbage truck, gas pump, golf ball, parachute) — reporting top-1 accuracy and latency. (Top-1 accuracy means the model’s single best guess was the right one; top-5 would mean the right answer was among its five best guesses. Latency is the time taken per image.)

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
Two paradigms
Two very different ways to label an image. A supervised model is trained on labelled examples of exactly the classes it predicts — show it thousands of tagged photos, and it learns to recognise those tags. A zero-shot model is used on categories it was never explicitly trained on, by description: you hand it the words for each class and it matches the picture to the words. The three models below come from two families (see Model families for the bigger picture):

ConvNeXt-Tiny — a modern image-recognition network (a “convolutional” one) that was trained the classic supervised way on ImageNet. timm is just the popular open-source library that ships it ready to use.
SigLIP 2 and OpenCLIP — CLIP-style models trained on huge numbers of image–caption pairs scraped from the web, so a photo and its caption end up described by similar lists of numbers. OpenCLIP is an open re-creation of OpenAI’s original CLIP; SigLIP 2 is a newer, refined version of the same idea.










Paradigm
Model here
How it decides
Changing the label set




Supervised
ConvNeXt-Tiny (timm)
A classification head trained on ImageNet outputs a score per class
Requires retraining the head


Zero-shot
SigLIP 2, OpenCLIP
Embed the image and the text "a photo of a {class}"; pick the closest text
Just edit the list of strings



This is the classification analogue of closed-set vs open-vocabulary detection from module 01. The supervised model has a fixed output space baked into its weights; the zero-shot models were trained on image–text pairs from the web and have never seen Imagenette’s task — yet they classify it by comparing the image to text descriptions.

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
How each model works

Supervised (ConvNeXt-Tiny). A modern convolutional backbone — the main body of the network that turns an image into useful features — with a 1000-way ImageNet head (the small final part that produces a score per class). To score Imagenette we simply read off the 10 relevant logits — the raw numeric scores a classifier outputs before picking the highest — no fine-tuning needed.
Zero-shot (CLIP / SigLIP 2). Two encoders — one for images, one for text — each turning its input into an embedding (a list of numbers, or vector, summarising it so similar things get similar lists). They are trained contrastively so that matching image/caption pairs land nearby in a shared space. Classification becomes: encode the image, encode each candidate label as a sentence, return the nearest. SigLIP 2 refines CLIP with a sigmoid loss (each pair judged independently) and stronger training, which generally lifts accuracy.

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
How we measured

Data: the full Imagenette validation split (3,925 images, 10 classes).
Metric: top-1 accuracy. For the supervised model, the prediction is the argmax over the 10 ImageNet logits that correspond to Imagenette’s classes; for zero-shot, the argmax over the 10 text-similarity scores.
Latency: mean of 30 warmed-up single-image forwards on the local RTX 3090 Ti.
Prompts for zero-shot are the simple template "a photo of a {class}." — prompt wording measurably affects zero-shot accuracy, so we fix it for a fair comparison.

Top-1 accuracy on 3925 Imagenette val images (10 classes). Latency = mean of 30 warmed-up single-image forwards on NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti | 24.0 GB | sm_86 | torch 2.11.0+cu128 / CUDA 12.8. Zero-shot models were never trained on Imagenette — they classify by image-text similarity.






NoteWhat to notice




Zero-shot is not a toy. SigLIP 2 (99.4%) and OpenCLIP (99.0%) land within a point of the supervised ConvNeXt-Tiny (99.9%) — despite never training on Imagenette. The decades-old assumption that you must train on your classes to classify them no longer holds for coarse, common categories.
Flexibility isn’t necessarily slower. The fastest model here is the zero-shot, permissively licensed OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 (99 fps, MIT) — and it’s within a point of the supervised model. (FPS = frames, i.e. images, processed per second; around 30 is fast enough for real-time video.) You can swap its label set by editing a list of strings.
SigLIP 2’s recipe shows. Its sigmoid-loss training edges OpenCLIP on accuracy (99.4 vs 99.0) at roughly half the throughput — a small, real accuracy/speed trade.
Imagenette is deliberately easy (10 distinct classes), so everything clusters above 99%. The takeaway isn’t the leaderboard order — it’s that the supervised advantage has nearly vanished on a task like this. On fine-grained or rare classes the gap reopens.

Predictions

title: 02 · Image classification
text:
Where classifiers fail

Fine-grained & ambiguous images — an object photographed oddly, or a scene containing several classes at once (classification must commit to one label).
Zero-shot prompt sensitivity — "a photo of a {class}" vs the bare class name vs an ensemble of templates can swing accuracy by points; the label wording is now a hyperparameter.
Distribution shift — accuracy on clean ImageNet-style photos overstates real-world performance on blurry, occluded, or domain-specific imagery.

title: 01 · Object detection — interactive walkthrough
text:
A hands-on companion to the report. Every model adapter returns a supervision.Detections, so the same few lines annotate any model’s output.
Requires the data + detection dependency groups, run from the repo root:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cvkit.devices import get_device, describe_device
from cvkit.io import load_image
from cvkit.viz import annotate_detections
import detect

device = get_device()
print(describe_device(device))

title: 01 · Object detection — interactive walkthrough
text:
Grab a few COCO images
Downloads (and caches) a small COCO val slice via the FiftyOne zoo.

from cvkit.data import load_coco_val_subset

ds = load_coco_val_subset(max_samples=8)
paths = [s.filepath for s in ds][:4]
img = load_image(paths[0])
plt.imshow(img); plt.axis('off'); plt.show()

title: 01 · Object detection — interactive walkthrough
text:
Run a closed-set detector (YOLO26)
Predicts one of the 80 COCO classes per box. data['class_name'] carries the labels.

model = detect.build_detector('yolo26n', device)
det = model.predict(img, conf=0.3)
labels = [f"{n} {c:.2f}" for n, c in zip(det.data.get('class_name', []), det.confidence)]
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.imshow(annotate_detections(img, det, labels=labels)); plt.axis('off'); plt.show()
print(list(det.data.get('class_name', [])))

title: 01 · Object detection — interactive walkthrough
section: Compare two models on the same image
text:
Compare two models on the same image
Same image, same code path — only the adapter key changes.

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 7))
for ax, key in zip(axes, ['yolo26n', 'rfdetr-nano']):
    m = detect.build_detector(key, device)
    d = m.predict(img, conf=0.3)
    lbls = [f"{n} {c:.2f}" for n, c in zip(d.data.get('class_name', []), d.confidence)]
    ax.imshow(annotate_detections(img, d, labels=lbls))
    ax.set_title(m.meta.display); ax.axis('off')
plt.show()

title: 01 · Object detection — interactive walkthrough
section: Open-vocabulary: detect anything you can name
text:
Open-vocabulary: detect anything you can name
Grounding DINO takes free text — try concepts that aren’t in COCO’s 80 classes.

gd = detect.build_open_vocab(device)
det = gd.detect(img, ['plate', 'napkin', 'logo', 'hand', 'cup'])
lbls = [f"{n} {c:.2f}" for n, c in zip(det.data.get('class_name', []), det.confidence)]
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.imshow(annotate_detections(img, det, labels=lbls)); plt.axis('off'); plt.show()
print('out-of-COCO:', sorted({n for n in det.data.get('class_name', []) if n.lower() not in detect.NAME_TO_IDX}))

title: CV Explorations
text:
This is a repo-as-curriculum: each major computer-vision capability is a self-contained module that is both runnable and explained. Every module ships a reusable CLI/engine, an interactive notebook, and a rigorous benchmark report — multiple models per task, real metrics, latency measured on the local GPU, and honest failure cases.
The reports you’re reading were rendered from results computed locally on an NVIDIA RTX 3090 Ti (24 GB); heavier or Linux-only work runs on an NVIDIA DGX Spark (ARM64 / GB10 Grace-Blackwell). Site rendering itself needs no GPU. The same code is validated on both x86 and ARM64 — see Cross-platform validation.

title: CV Explorations
text:
A useful mental model
The biggest shift in modern CV is task-specific models → promptable foundation models:

Old way: train one model per task on a fixed label set.
New way: one model you prompt — SAM (“segment whatever I point at”), Grounding DINO (“detect anything I name in text”), CLIP (“classify against any labels”), Florence-2 (“detect, caption, OCR, and ground from one checkpoint”).

Each module shows both, side by side, so the why of the modern approach is visible rather than asserted.

title: CV Explorations
text:
How to run it yourself
uv sync --group dev                # core env (PyTorch + CV stack)
uv sync --group data --group detection   # extras for the detection module
uv run python modules/01-object-detection/run.py --help
See the repository README for the full setup and hardware notes.

title: Foundations: start here
text: If you’re new to computer vision (or to machine learning, or even to a lot of programming), read this page first. It builds up — from “what is an image to a computer” to “how do these models work and how do we measure them” — every idea the module reports lean on. Nothing here assumes you’ve seen it before. There’s a one-line glossary for quick lookups and a model families page that introduces each system by name (YOLO, DINO, SAM, …).

title: Foundations: start here
text:
What is computer vision?
Computer vision (CV) is the field of getting computers to extract meaning from images and video — the way your eyes and brain do without effort. “Is there a person in this photo? Where? How far away? What are they doing?” are all trivial for you and were, until recently, nearly impossible for a computer. This site is a tour of the modern techniques that changed that.

title: Foundations: start here
section: How does a computer “see” an image?
text:
How does a computer “see” an image?
A computer has no eyes. To it, an image is just a big grid of numbers.

A digital image is a grid of pixels (picture elements). A phone photo might be 4000 × 3000 pixels — 12 million of them.
Each pixel has a colour, stored as three numbers: how much Red, Green, and Blue it contains, each from 0 to 255. White is (255, 255, 255); pure red is (255, 0, 0). These three are called channels.
So a colour image is really a stack of three grids of numbers — height × width × 3. That block of numbers is called a tensor (just a fancy word for a multi-dimensional array of numbers).

That’s the entire input. Every model on this site takes in that grid of numbers and outputs other numbers — a label, box coordinates, a depth value per pixel. All of computer vision is turning one set of numbers into another, usefully.

title: Foundations: start here
text:
What is a “model”?
A model is a mathematical function with a huge number of adjustable knobs. You feed numbers in (the image), it does a long chain of arithmetic, and numbers come out (the answer). The knobs — there can be millions or billions of them — are called parameters or weights.
The magic is how the knobs get set. Nobody sets them by hand. Instead:

A neural network is a particular kind of model, loosely inspired by brain neurons: it’s built from many simple units arranged in layers. Early layers detect simple things (edges, colours); later layers combine those into complex things (an eye, a wheel, a face). “Deep learning” just means a neural network with many layers.
The weights start out random — the model knows nothing. Then we train it.


Training vs. inference (the two phases)
This distinction is everywhere, so it’s worth nailing:

Training is the learning phase. We show the model thousands of examples for which we already know the right answer (e.g. images each labelled “cat” or “dog” — those known answers are called labels or ground truth). Each time the model guesses, we measure how wrong it was (the loss) and nudge every weight a tiny bit in the direction that would have made it less wrong. Repeat millions of times and the random knobs slowly become a working cat-vs-dog detector. The nudging algorithm is gradient descent; one full pass over the training data is an epoch.
Inference is the using phase. Training is done, the weights are frozen, and you just feed in a new image to get an answer. Inference is fast (milliseconds); training is slow (minutes to weeks).

A model whose weights have already been trained (often by a big lab, on millions of images) is pretrained. A saved set of trained weights is a checkpoint (you’ll see files like yolo26n.pt). Almost everything on this site uses pretrained models — you download someone’s finished weights and run inference. Module 10 is the exception: it trains (specifically, fine-tunes — see below).


Why GPUs?
All this arithmetic is the same simple operation (multiply-and-add) done billions of times in parallel. A GPU (graphics card) is hardware built to do exactly that, thousands of operations at once, so it runs these models 10–100× faster than a normal CPU. The model’s weights have to fit in the GPU’s memory (VRAM); that’s why you’ll see “24 GB” mentioned — it caps how big a model you can run. Everything here runs on a 24 GB NVIDIA RTX 3090 Ti.

title: Foundations: start here
text:
The two great architectures
Almost every model here is built on one of two designs (or a mix). You don’t need the math, just the intuition.

CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks)
A CNN is the classic image architecture (dominant from 2012). Its key trick is the convolution: it slides a small window (a filter) across the image looking for one pattern — say, a vertical edge — and reports where it found it. Stack many of these: the first layer finds edges, the next combines edges into textures and corners, the next into parts (an eye, a wheel), the next into whole objects. Because the same small filter is reused across the whole image, CNNs are efficient and naturally handle “the cat could be anywhere in the frame.” YOLO (modules 01, 03, 05, 06) is the famous CNN-family detector.


Transformers and “attention” (and ViTs)
The transformer (from 2017, originally for language — it’s the “T” in ChatGPT) now dominates vision too. Its key trick is attention: every part of the input can directly “look at” every other part and decide what’s relevant. For an image, a Vision Transformer (ViT) chops the picture into a grid of small patches, turns each into numbers, and lets all patches attend to one another — so the model can relate a hand on one side of the image to a tool on the other in a single step. CNNs build understanding up locally; transformers can use the whole image’s context at once. DETR / RF-DETR (module 01), DINO (module 07), SAM (module 03), and the vision-language models (module 08) are all transformer-based.


Backbone vs. head
A common pattern: a backbone (a CNN or ViT) chews the image into a rich numerical summary of “what’s in it” (called features or an embedding), and a small head on top turns those features into the specific answer for your task (a class, boxes, a depth map). Swap the head, reuse the backbone, and you can do a different task — which is why one good backbone (like DINO) shows up behind many models.

title: Foundations: start here
section: The core tasks (one per kind of question)
text:
The core tasks (one per kind of question)
Each module answers a different question about an image. From simplest to richest:



Task
The question
Output
Module




Classification

01


Segmentation

Tracking

A bounding box (“box”) is just a rectangle around an object, given as four numbers (left, top, right, bottom). A mask is a yes/no for every pixel (“is this pixel part of the dog?”). A keypoint is a single marked point (e.g. “left elbow”).

title: Foundations: start here
text:
How we measure success (metrics)
A core promise of this site is honest numbers, so you need to know what they mean.
For classification:

Accuracy — the fraction it got right. Top-1 accuracy = the model’s single best guess was correct. Top-5 accuracy = the right answer was among its top five guesses (useful when many classes look alike).

For detection (and anything with boxes):

IoU (Intersection over Union) — how well a predicted box overlaps the true box. Imagine the two rectangles; IoU = (area where they overlap) ÷ (area they cover together). 1.0 = perfect overlap, 0 = no overlap. A prediction usually “counts” if IoU ≥ 0.5.
Precision — of the boxes the model predicted, what fraction were correct? (Penalises false positives — crying wolf.)
Recall — of the objects that were really there, what fraction did the model find? (Penalises false negatives — misses.)
There’s always a trade-off: a model can catch everything by guessing wildly (high recall, low precision) or only report sure things (high precision, low recall). A confidence score (the model’s own 0–1 certainty per prediction) and a threshold control where you sit.
mAP (mean Average Precision) — the standard single detection score, from 0 to 1 (often shown as a %). It sweeps the confidence threshold to trace the full precision-vs-recall curve, takes the area under it (that’s Average Precision), and averages across all object classes. COCO mAP averages this over ten overlap strictnesses (IoU 0.5 to 0.95), so it rewards tight boxes, not just roughly-right ones. Higher = better; ~0.5 is strong for everyday photos.

For speed:

Latency — how long one image takes, in milliseconds (ms). Lower = faster.
FPS (frames per second) / throughput — how many images per second. ~30 FPS is real-time video. Latency and FPS are two views of the same thing (30 FPS ≈ 33 ms each).

Accuracy and speed usually trade off against each other — a recurring theme in the reports is that there’s no single “best” model, only different points on that curve.

title: Foundations: start here
text:
Jargon you’ll meet everywhere
Short plain-language versions (the glossary has the rest):

NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression) — detectors often fire several overlapping boxes on one object. NMS is the clean-up step that keeps the most confident box and deletes its duplicates. “NMS-free” (e.g. YOLO26) means the model learned to emit one box per object directly, skipping that step — simpler and faster.
Anchor — older detectors guessed by adjusting a fixed set of pre-defined template boxes (“anchors”) tiled over the image. “Anchor-free” models drop that crutch and predict boxes directly.
Embedding — a list of numbers (a vector) that summarises an image (or patch, or word) so that similar things get similar vectors. Comparing images becomes comparing vectors. The heart of module 07.
Zero-shot — using a model on a task or set of categories it was never explicitly trained on, by description rather than by examples. CLIP can classify “a photo of a tractor” without ever having a “tractor” training label (module 02).
Open-vocabulary — a detector/segmenter you prompt with free text (“find the forklift”) instead of a fixed list of classes (module 01).
Foundation model — a large model pretrained on enormous, broad data, meant to be reused for many tasks (often by prompting), rather than trained for one. SAM, CLIP, and the VLMs are examples.
Fine-tuning — taking a pretrained model and training it a bit more on your specific data to specialise it (cheaper than training from scratch). Module 10.
Augmentation — making more training examples by randomly flipping/cropping/recolouring the ones you have, so the model generalises better.
Inference vs training, weights/checkpoint, epoch, VRAM — defined above.

title: Foundations: start here
section: The big arc of the field (why the models are the way they are)
text:
The big arc of the field (why the models are the way they are)
This whole site is, in a sense, a history in fast-forward:

Classical CV (pre-2012). Humans hand-designed the rules — explicit math to find edges, corners, and motion. Fast and transparent, but blind to meaning (“edge” yes, “cat” no). This is module 09, shown last as the illuminating contrast.
The deep-learning revolution (2012). A CNN crushed the ImageNet image-classification contest, and the field switched almost overnight from hand-designed rules to learned features. Accuracy leapt.
The transformer era (2017→). Attention let models use global context and scale to enormous data, taking over both language and vision.
Foundation & promptable models (2021→). Instead of one model per task trained on a fixed label list, we got big models you prompt: CLIP (classify by text), SAM (segment anything you point at), Grounding DINO (detect anything you name). This is the single biggest shift the reports keep returning to: task-specific models → promptable foundation models.
Vision-language models (2023→). Bolt a vision system onto a language model and you can simply ask an image questions in English (module 08).

Each module shows the modern approach next to what it replaced, so you see why the field moved.

title: Foundations: start here
section: Why licences appear in the tables
text:
Why licences appear in the tables
Open-source models come with legal terms. Some are permissive (Apache-2.0, MIT — free to use, including commercially). Some are copyleft (AGPL-3.0 — free to learn with, but commercial use requires either open-sourcing your own code or buying a licence). The reports record each model’s licence because for real projects it matters as much as the accuracy — and noticing it is part of becoming a practitioner.

title: Foundations: start here
section: How to read the rest of this site
text:
How to read the rest of this site
Each module is a self-contained report with the same shape: the task in plain terms → the models (see model families for who’s who) → how we measured → the results with annotated pictures → where it fails. A good order if you’re new:

02 · Classification — the simplest task; eases you in.
01 · Object detection — the iconic task; sets the template.
Then follow your curiosity through 03–10.

When a word stops you, check the glossary. When a model name stops you, check model families. Welcome — you’re going to understand all of it.

title: Cross-platform validation (DGX Spark)
text: A goal of this repo is that the code is portable, not tied to one machine. The modules are developed on an x86-64 workstation with an RTX 3090 Ti (Ampere, CUDA 12.8). To prove they aren’t accidentally x86-only, the same code was validated on a very different machine — an NVIDIA DGX Spark: ARM64 (aarch64), GB10 Grace-Blackwell GPU, sm_121, CUDA 13, unified memory. (The Spark also does robotics duty, which is why depth / pose / tracking / segmentation matter here — they’re robot-perception primitives.)

title: Cross-platform validation (DGX Spark)
section: The one thing that changes: the PyTorch build
text:
The one thing that changes: the PyTorch build
Everything in this repo is pure-Python or ships ARM64 wheels (ultralytics, supervision, transformers, timm, opencv…). The single platform-specific dependency is PyTorch + CUDA:

On the x86 workstation, pyproject.toml pins the CUDA 12.8 wheels (Ampere-friendly).
On the ARM64 Spark, that pin is wrong — sm_121 needs CUDA 13. The clean fix is to run inside NVIDIA’s NGC PyTorch container (nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch), which ships a Blackwell-ready torch for aarch64, and create a venv with --system-site-packages so the repo’s extra deps install on top of that torch rather than dragging in an incompatible build.

That’s the whole portability story: swap the torch build, keep the code.

title: Cross-platform validation (DGX Spark)
section: What was validated on the GB10
text:
What was validated on the GB10
Cloning this public repo into the NGC container and running it produced:
device: NVIDIA GB10 | 121.7 GB | sm_121 | torch 2.11.0a0 (nv) / CUDA 13.2

cvkit unit tests ......... 6 passed
yolo26n via modules/01 detect.py:
  detections: 5  ->  ['bus', 'person', 'person', 'person', 'person']

cvkit is platform-agnostic — its device/IO/timing/metrics helpers pass unchanged on ARM64, and describe_device correctly reports the GB10 at sm_121 / CUDA 13.2.
The module adapters run as-is — the exact detect.py used on the 3090 Ti loads YOLO26 and produces correct detections on Blackwell, no code edits.

title: Cross-platform validation (DGX Spark)
text:
Takeaways

A repo like this is portable if you isolate the torch build and keep everything else pure Python / wheel-installable. The cvkit abstraction (one device/dtype path, no hard-coded CUDA assumptions) is what makes that cheap.
The NGC container is the path of least resistance on bleeding-edge hardware (new sm_, new CUDA), versus assembling pip wheels by hand.
Heavier or Linux-only tooling (mmdet/detectron2/flash-attention) would also live on the Spark rather than the Windows workstation — the natural division of labour for this project.

title: Glossary
text: New here? The Foundations page explains these in context and in order. This page is for quick lookups. Terms are grouped, then alphabetical within a group.

title: Glossary
text:
Core concepts
Annotation — a label drawn onto data (a box, mask, or class) marking the correct answer; also the act of drawing it.
Backbone — the main body of a network that turns an image into a rich numerical summary (features); a small “head” on top produces the task-specific answer.
Channel — one colour layer of an image. Colour images have three (Red, Green, Blue).
Checkpoint — a saved file of a model’s trained weights (e.g. yolo26n.pt). Load it to skip training and run the model.
CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) — the classic image architecture; slides small pattern filters across the image, building edges → textures → parts → objects.
Deep learning — machine learning with neural networks that have many layers.
Embedding (vector) — a list of numbers summarising an image/patch/word such that similar things get similar lists. Comparing things becomes comparing vectors.
Epoch — one full pass of training over the entire training dataset.
Feature — a piece of information a model has extracted from the input (e.g. “there’s an edge here,” or, deeper, “this looks like fur”).
Fine-tuning — continuing to train a pretrained model on your own smaller dataset to specialise it. Much cheaper than training from scratch.
Ground truth — the known-correct answer for an example, used to train or score a model.
GPU / VRAM — the graphics processor that runs these models fast by doing massive parallel math; VRAM is its memory, which limits how big a model fits (here, 24 GB).
Head — the small final part of a network that converts backbone features into the task output (a class, boxes, a depth map…).
Inference — running a trained model to get answers on new inputs. (The “use” phase, vs. training.)
Label — the correct answer attached to a training example (e.g. “cat”).
Latency — time to process one input, usually in milliseconds (ms). Lower is faster.
Layer — one stage of a neural network; data flows through many in sequence.
Loss — a number measuring how wrong the model’s prediction is; training works to make it small.
Model — a mathematical function with many adjustable numbers (weights) that maps an input to an output.
Neural network — a model built from many simple units in layers, loosely inspired by neurons.
Parameter / weight — one of the millions of adjustable numbers inside a model; training sets their values.
Pretrained — a model whose weights were already trained (often by a big lab on huge data), ready to download and use.
Tensor — a multi-dimensional grid of numbers; an image is a height × width × 3 tensor.
Training — the learning phase: adjusting weights from labelled examples so the model gets less wrong over time.
Transformer — an architecture built on “attention,” where every part of the input can directly relate to every other part; now dominant in vision and language.
Attention — the transformer’s core operation: letting each element decide which other elements are relevant to it.
ViT (Vision Transformer) — a transformer applied to images by splitting the picture into patches and letting them attend to each other.

title: Glossary
text:
Tasks
Classification — assign one label to a whole image.
Object detection — find objects and draw a labelled box around each.
Segmentation — label every pixel; instance segmentation separates individual objects, semantic segmentation labels by category.
Depth estimation — predict how far each pixel is from the camera. Monocular = from a single image.
Pose estimation / keypoints — locate body joints and connect them into a skeleton.
Tracking (MOT, Multi-Object Tracking) — follow the same objects across video frames, giving each a persistent ID.
Retrieval — find the images most similar to a query, using embeddings.
VLM (Vision-Language Model) — a model that takes an image (and text) and produces text: captions, answers, reasoning.

title: Glossary
text:
Detection & evaluation terms
Anchor — a pre-defined template box older detectors adjust to fit objects; “anchor-free” models skip them.
Bounding box (box) — a rectangle around an object, given as four numbers (left, top, right, bottom).
Confidence — the model’s own 0–1 certainty in a prediction; you threshold it to decide what to keep.
COCO — a large standard dataset of everyday photos (80 object classes) used to train and compare detectors. “COCO mAP” is the standard detection score.
False positive / false negative — a wrong detection (saw something that wasn’t there) / a miss (failed to see something that was).
FPS (frames per second) / throughput — how many images a model processes per second; ~30 FPS is real-time.
IoU (Intersection over Union) — overlap between a predicted and true box: shared area ÷ combined area. 1 = perfect.
Keypoint — a single labelled point, e.g. “right knee,” in pose estimation.
mAP (mean Average Precision) — the standard detection score (0–1): area under the precision–recall curve, averaged over classes (and, for COCO, over IoU thresholds 0.5–0.95).
Mask — a per-pixel yes/no marking exactly which pixels belong to an object.
NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression) — clean-up step that removes duplicate overlapping boxes for the same object, keeping the most confident. “NMS-free” models avoid needing it.
OKS (Object Keypoint Similarity) — the IoU-equivalent for pose: how well predicted joints match the true ones; the basis of pose mAP.
Precision — of the model’s predictions, the fraction that were correct (penalises false alarms).
Recall — of the real objects, the fraction the model found (penalises misses).
mIoU (mean IoU) — the standard segmentation score: average pixel-overlap across classes.
HOTA / MOTA / IDF1 — tracking scores measuring how well IDs stay correct across frames; ID-switch = a tracked object wrongly getting a new ID.

title: Glossary
text:
Modern / foundation-model terms
CLIP — a model trained to put matching images and text captions near each other in one embedding space, enabling zero-shot classification and retrieval.
Foundation model — a large model pretrained broadly and reused for many tasks, often by prompting, instead of trained for one task.
Open-vocabulary — detect/segment by free-text prompt (“find the ladder”) rather than a fixed class list.
Prompt — the instruction you give a promptable model: text (CLIP, VLMs, Grounding DINO), a point or box (SAM), etc.
Pseudo-label — a label produced by another model instead of a human; cheap but noisier. Used to auto-build training data in module 10.
Zero-shot — applying a model to categories/tasks it was never explicitly trained on, via description.

title: Glossary
text:
Tooling you’ll see named
ultralytics — the popular library that packages YOLO models for easy training and inference (AGPL-3.0 licensed).
transformers (Hugging Face) — the standard library for downloading and running a huge range of models with one API.
supervision — a utility library used here to hold detections/keypoints and draw them on images.
FiftyOne — a tool for downloading, browsing, and evaluating datasets (used to fetch COCO subsets).
Quarto — the tool that turns these reports into the website you’re reading.
uv — the Python package/environment manager used to install everything reproducibly.

title: Model families: who’s who
text: The reports mention a lot of model names — YOLO, RF-DETR, SAM, CLIP, DINO… If those are just unfamiliar words, this page is for you. Each entry says what it is, the key idea, and where it appears here. For the underlying concepts (CNN, transformer, embedding, mAP), see Foundations.

title: Model families: who’s who
text:
Detection & segmentation models

YOLO (“You Only Look Once”)

What: the most famous real-time object detector family; a CNN that finds and labels objects in one fast pass. Now maintained by the company Ultralytics; the current generation is YOLO26 (2026), with YOLO11 the prior one. Versions come in sizes n/s/m/l/x (nano → extra large); “n” is tiny and fast.
Key idea: look at the whole image once and predict all boxes simultaneously (hence the name) — that’s what makes it fast enough for video. YOLO26 is NMS-free (it emits one clean box per object without the usual duplicate-removal step) and anchor-free (no template boxes).
Licence: AGPL-3.0 (free to learn with; commercial use has conditions).
Where: 01 detection, 03 segmentation, 05 pose, 06 tracking, 10 fine-tuning. YOLO is the workhorse of the site.



DETR and RF-DETR

What: DETR (“DEtection TRansformer”) is a different way to do object detection, using a transformer instead of the YOLO-style CNN pipeline. RF-DETR (by Roboflow, 2025) is a modern, fast, accurate member of this family and the permissively-licensed (Apache-2.0) accuracy leader in module 01.
Key idea: treat detection as set prediction — the model has a fixed set of “object queries,” each of which learns to go find one object. Because it’s designed to output a clean set directly, it needs no anchors and no NMS. Built on a transformer backbone (RF-DETR uses DINOv2, below).
Where: 01 detection.



SAM (“Segment Anything Model”)

What: Meta’s promptable segmentation foundation model — it outlines the exact pixels of whatever you point at. SAM 2.1 is used here; SAM 3 is the newest. Apache-2.0 (SAM 2.1).
Key idea: instead of a fixed list of classes, you give it a prompt — a click, a box, or (in SAM 3) a text phrase — and it returns a precise mask. It has no notion of what the thing is (“object-ness,” not “cat”), which is exactly why it generalises to objects no labelled detector knows.
Where: 03 segmentation.



Grounding DINO

What: an open-vocabulary detector — you give it free text and it draws boxes around whatever you named, even categories no fixed-list detector was trained on. (Unrelated to the DINO backbone below despite the shared name.) Apache-2.0.
Key idea: it fuses a detector with a language understanding of your prompt, so “a person wearing a hard hat” becomes boxes without any retraining.
Where: 01 detection (the open-vocabulary demo) and as the auto-labeller in 10 fine-tuning.

title: Model families: who’s who
text:
Backbones & embedding models

DINO / DINOv2

What: Meta’s self-supervised image backbone — a Vision Transformer that learned to produce excellent general-purpose image embeddings without any labels at all. DINOv2 is the version used here (Apache-2.0).
Key idea: “self-supervised” means it taught itself by making different cropped/altered views of the same photo agree with each other — no human labels needed. The resulting features are so strong they power retrieval, and even serve as the backbone inside other models (RF-DETR uses DINOv2).
Where: 07 retrieval; inside RF-DETR in module 01.



CLIP / SigLIP / OpenCLIP

What: vision-language embedding models that put images and text into the same space, so you can compare a picture to a sentence. CLIP (OpenAI) started it; OpenCLIP is an open re-implementation; SigLIP 2 (Google) is a stronger modern version. All permissive.
Key idea: trained on hundreds of millions of (image, caption) pairs to make a photo’s vector land near its caption’s vector. This unlocks zero-shot classification (score an image against “a photo of a {class}”) and text-image retrieval — no task-specific training.
Where: 02 classification (zero-shot), 07 retrieval, and as the relevance filter in 10 fine-tuning.



ConvNeXt & timm

What: ConvNeXt is a modern, high-performance CNN image classifier. timm is the popular library that hosts hundreds of pretrained classification backbones like it. Apache-2.0.
Key idea: ConvNeXt shows a well-designed CNN can still match transformers on classification — a useful “supervised baseline” to compare zero-shot models against.
Where: 02 classification.

title: Model families: who’s who
text:
Depth, and other specialists

Depth Anything

What: a monocular depth foundation model — estimates how far away every pixel is from a single ordinary photo. Depth Anything V2 (S/B sizes) is used here; Depth Anything 3 is newest. Built on a DINOv2 backbone. Apache-2.0 (small/base).
Key idea: trained on a huge mix of real and auto-generated depth data, it reads the monocular cues humans use (perspective, relative size, occlusion) to turn one flat image into 3-D structure — the perception primitive most relevant to robotics.
Where: 04 depth.



RTMPose / ViTPose (pose)

What: dedicated pose-estimation models (mentioned as the accuracy-first alternative to YOLO-pose). They take the “top-down” approach: first detect each person, then run a specialised network on each to place the joints.
Where: discussed in 05 pose (which benchmarks the faster single-stage YOLO-pose).



ByteTrack (tracking)

What: not a neural network but a tracking algorithm — it links a detector’s boxes across video frames so each object keeps a stable ID. MIT-licensed.
Key idea: predict where each tracked object should move next (a simple motion model) and match it to the new frame’s detections — cleverly using even low-confidence detections to bridge brief occlusions. This is “tracking-by-detection”: the detector finds, ByteTrack remembers.
Where: 06 tracking.

title: Model families: who’s who
text:
Vision-language models (VLMs)

Qwen2.5-VL (and Florence-2, Moondream, …)

What: vision-language models — a vision encoder bolted onto a large language model, so you can ask an image questions in plain English and get text back. Qwen2.5-VL (Alibaba, Apache-2.0) is used here. Florence-2 (Microsoft) is a tiny unified alternative; Moondream, PaliGemma are others.
Key idea: the image is converted into tokens the language model can “read,” so a single model can caption, answer questions, count, read text, and reason — whichever you ask for in the prompt. The most general capability on the site, and the slowest.
Where: 08 vision-language models.

title: Model families: who’s who
text:
Classical (pre-deep-learning) techniques

OpenCV: Canny, ORB, optical flow

What: OpenCV is the standard library of classical, hand-designed vision algorithms — no training, no neural networks. Canny finds edges; ORB finds and matches distinctive points between images; optical flow estimates pixel motion between video frames. MIT/BSD.
Key idea: explicit math, not learning — fast, transparent, and still the backbone of camera calibration, panorama stitching, and the geometry front-end of robot navigation (SLAM).
Where: 09 classical CV — the contrast that makes the learned models legible.


Licences here are summarised for orientation; each module’s results table records the exact licence of each model it benchmarks, and why it matters for real use.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
The task
Object detection answers two questions at once: what objects are present and where they are, as axis-aligned bounding boxes (a bounding box is just a rectangle drawn around an object — four numbers: left, top, right, bottom) with class labels and confidence scores (the model’s own 0–1 certainty in each prediction). It sits between classification (one label for the whole image) and segmentation (a label for every pixel), and it underpins most real-world perception — including the robotics work this repo’s hardware also serves.
The headline metric is mean Average Precision (mAP) — the standard detection score from 0 to 1, where higher is better: for each class you sweep a confidence threshold to trace a precision–recall curve (precision = of the model’s predictions, the fraction that were correct; recall = of the real objects, the fraction the model found), take the area under it (Average Precision), and average across classes. COCO (a standard 80-class dataset of everyday photos used to train and compare detectors) reports mAP averaged over ten IoU thresholds from 0.50 to 0.95 (written mAP@[.50:.95]) — IoU, or Intersection over Union, measures how much a predicted box overlaps the true box (shared area ÷ combined area, so 1.0 is a perfect match), which rewards tight boxes, not just roughly-right ones.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
Two paradigms
The most important idea to carry across this whole curriculum is the split between closed-set and open-vocabulary detection:

Closed-set models (YOLO, RF-DETR here) predict from a fixed label set — the 80 COCO classes. Fast, accurate, benchmarkable, but blind to anything off-list.
Open-vocabulary models (Grounding DINO, YOLO-World) take text at inference time and detect whatever you name — “a forklift”, “a person wearing hi-vis” — with no retraining. More flexible, generally slower and lower-AP on the fixed COCO set. (Open-vocabulary means detecting by free-text prompt instead of from a fixed class list.)

For background on each model named here, see Model families.
This report benchmarks the closed-set models head-to-head (they share the COCO label space, so mAP is a fair comparison), then demonstrates the open-vocabulary alternative qualitatively below — it isn’t restricted to the 80 COCO names, so a like-for-like mAP wouldn’t be meaningful.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
The architectures benchmarked
The two families below take different routes to the same goal. YOLO26 is the newest of the fast, real-time YOLO detectors; RF-DETR is a transformer-based detector that trades a little speed for accuracy. Two architecture words recur: a CNN slides small pattern-matching filters across the image (the classic image architecture), while a transformer instead relates every part of the image to every other part (an idea called “attention”).









Family
Member(s)
How it localises
Notable trait




YOLO
YOLO26-n, YOLO11-n
Dense grid of predictions over a CNN/feature pyramid
YOLO26 is NMS-free — it learns to emit one box per object, removing the non-maximum-suppression (NMS, a clean-up step that deletes duplicate overlapping boxes for the same object and keeps the most confident) post-step that older YOLOs need


RF-DETR
Nano, Medium
A transformer decoder with a fixed set of learned “object queries” (each query is one “slot” the model uses to find one object), each attending to the image
DETR-style; no anchors (older detectors nudge pre-set template boxes called anchors; anchor-free models predict boxes directly), no NMS by design; built on a DINOv2 backbone (the backbone is the main body of the network that turns an image into features)



The interesting contrast: YOLO descends from dense, anchor-based CNN detectors that were engineered to suppress duplicates; DETR-family models reframe detection as set prediction and learn to avoid duplicates end-to-end. YOLO26 narrows that gap by going NMS-free too.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
How we measured
Rigour notes, so the numbers mean something:

Data: a fixed 200-image slice of COCO val2017 (downloaded via the FiftyOne zoo). Ground-truth iscrowd regions are excluded, matching COCO’s ignore policy.
mAP: computed with torchmetrics (pycocotools-equivalent) at IoU=.50:.95. Inference is run at a near-zero confidence threshold (~0.001) so the full precision–recall curve is captured — thresholding higher silently understates mAP.
Class identity is matched by name across models, because YOLO uses COCO ids 0–79 while RF-DETR uses the original 1–90 ids; comparing raw ids would be wrong.
Latency: mean of 30 warmed-up, single-image predict() calls on the local RTX 3090 Ti — end-to-end (pre-process → forward → post-process), i.e. the latency you’d actually feel, not just the GPU kernel time.

NoteWhat to notice




Accuracy vs speed is a Pareto frontier, not a ranking. RF-DETR Medium tops accuracy (59.4 mAP) at ~27 fps; YOLO26-n gives up ~16 points of mAP (43.0) to nearly double the throughput (53 fps). Neither is “best” — they sit at different points on the curve.
A new generation earns its number. YOLO26-n beats prior-gen YOLO11-n by +5.4 mAP (43.0 vs 37.6) at essentially the same speed — and does it NMS-free.
At batch = 1, big models can be latency-free. RF-DETR Medium and Nano measured ~36 ms either way: single-image inference is dominated by fixed pre/post-process overhead, so Medium buys +8.1 mAP for ~no extra latency. (This flips with batching, where the heavier backbone’s compute dominates.)
mAP (small) is everyone’s weak spot, and the relative gap is widest there: 31.6 vs 17.9 between best and worst — a ~1.8× spread, larger than the overall-mAP spread.
License is a real axis. The most accurate model here (RF-DETR, Apache-2.0) is also the permissive one; the fastest (YOLO, AGPL-3.0) carries copyleft terms that matter the moment this leaves a learning context.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
Open-vocabulary in action
The four models above can only ever emit one of the 80 COCO names. Grounding DINO (an open-vocabulary detector that matches free-text phrases to regions of the image) removes that ceiling: you hand it a sentence of phrases at inference time and it localises whatever it can find — no retraining, no fixed label set.
Run on the same kind of images with the prompt person. bottle. cup. plate. napkin. logo. shoe. hand., it returns not just the COCO classes but plate, napkin, logo, shoe, hand — concepts that don’t exist in COCO’s vocabulary at all:






Figure 5: Grounding DINO — open-vocabulary detection. The prompt mixes COCO and non-COCO concepts; the out-of-COCO hits are what a closed-set model can never produce.









TipThe trade-off



Open-vocabulary buys flexibility at a price: Grounding DINO is slower than the YOLO/RF-DETR detectors, and you can’t quote a single COCO mAP for it (its label space is unbounded). The practical pattern is to use an open-vocab model to find or auto-label novel classes, then distil a fast closed-set detector for deployment — a thread later modules pick up.

title: 01 · Object detection
text:
Where detectors fail
Worth looking for in the figures above and your own images:

Small & crowded objects — distant people, stacked items; the mAP (small) column quantifies this.
Domain shift — COCO is everyday photos; performance drops on aerial, medical, industrial, or robot-camera imagery the models never trained on.
Class confusion — semantically close categories (couch vs chair, truck vs car).
Confidence calibration — a high score is not a guarantee; thresholds must be tuned per deployment.

title: 02 · Classification — interactive walkthrough
section: The zero-shot super-power: classify against any labels
text:
The zero-shot super-power: classify against any labels
No retraining — just change the strings. Try labels that aren’t Imagenette classes at all.

import open_clip, torch
from PIL import Image
m, _, pre = open_clip.create_model_and_transforms('ViT-B-32', pretrained='laion2b_s34b_b79k')
m = m.to(device).eval(); tok = open_clip.get_tokenizer('ViT-B-32')

labels = ['a fish', 'a dog', 'a musical instrument', 'a vehicle', 'a building', 'sports equipment']
with torch.no_grad():
    tf = m.encode_text(tok([f'a photo of {l}' for l in labels]).to(device)); tf /= tf.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    ie = m.encode_image(pre(Image.fromarray(img)).unsqueeze(0).to(device)); ie /= ie.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    sims = (ie @ tf.T)[0]
for l, s in sorted(zip(labels, sims.tolist()), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
    print(f'{s:.3f}  {l}')

title: 09 · Classical computer vision
text:
NoteNew here? Read this first





No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you’ve skimmed Foundations (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we measure success). Stuck on a word? See the Glossary. Stuck on a model name? See Model families. Key terms are explained inline the first time they appear.

title: 09 · Classical computer vision
text:
Why this module exists
Modules 01–08 are all neural networks. This one deliberately goes back to the pre-deep-learning toolkit — classical CV, meaning hand-designed algorithms (explicit math), with no training and no neural networks. These come from OpenCV (a free, widely used library of ready-made image-processing functions; see Model families) and are fast, transparent, deterministic, and need no training data or GPU. Deterministic means the same input always gives the same output — no randomness, no learned weights. They aren’t a historical curiosity — they still run inside camera calibration, visual SLAM front-ends (SLAM = building a map and tracking a camera’s position as it moves, using these classical features), image stabilisation, panorama stitching, and as cheap preprocessing for the deep models. Seeing them makes the rest of the curriculum legible: you can read every line of what these do, which is rarely true of a learned model.

title: 09 · Classical computer vision
text:
Four classics

Edge detection & contours
Edge detection means finding the outlines where image brightness changes sharply. The Canny edge detector (a classic edge-finding recipe; see Model families) finds intensity discontinuities (gradients), then thins and links them into clean edges. A gradient is just how fast brightness changes from pixel to pixel — that change is what an edge detector looks for. Contours chain those edges into closed boundaries you can measure (area, perimeter, shape); a contour is a connected boundary line traced around a shape. For decades this was “object detection”.






Figure 1: Canny edges and extracted contours — pure gradient math, no learning.





Feature detection & matching (ORB)
ORB (a fast classical keypoint finder; see Model families) finds repeatable keypoints (corners) and encodes each as a binary descriptor, so the same physical point can be matched across two images. Here a feature or keypoint is a distinctive point — like a corner — that can be re-found in another image; a descriptor is the short numeric fingerprint of that keypoint used to match it across images; and feature matching is pairing the same physical point between two images. Matching keypoints between views is the engine behind panorama stitching, structure-from-motion, and the front-end of visual SLAM — the hand-built ancestor of the learned embeddings in module 07. (A related classical detector you may see named is SIFT, an older scale-invariant keypoint finder that ORB was designed to replace more cheaply.)






Figure 2: ORB keypoints matched between two video frames.





Optical flow
Optical flow is the per-pixel motion between two video frames — its direction and speed. Farneback dense optical flow (a classical algorithm that computes this flow for every pixel; see Model families) estimates a motion vector for every pixel between two frames — direction as hue, speed as brightness. It’s geometry, not recognition: no idea what is moving, only how. Flow feeds stabilisation, motion segmentation, and action recognition.






Figure 3: Dense optical flow between two frames.

text: Results

Classical CV: operation timings (CPU)



Operation
